If all five premises are true at once, then the scene is a discussion about how discovery works under a severe constraint: some inputs contribute to reasoning and some do not obviously do so, yet both are present in the same world.

First, creative progress cannot be modeled as a simple narrowing toward one answer. If progress alternates between expansion and compression, then periods of good work must sometimes increase the number of live possibilities before later reducing them. A researcher who only compresses too early risks cutting off the very reframings that would have made better structure visible. So “collapse the search space” is at best incomplete. Search sometimes has to be re-opened.

Second, if reframing is the scarce resource, then the bottleneck is not mainly generating more candidate statements within a fixed ontology. It is changing the ontology itself: deciding what even counts as an object, a relation, or a property. In that world, two investigators with equal logic and equal evidence can differ greatly because one can redescribe the problem and the other cannot. Compression after a fruitful reframe may be powerful, but the reframe is rarer and more decisive.

Third, if grammar and coherence filters may not help much, then surface well-formedness is weak evidence of explanatory value. Most bad scientific explanations already pass those filters. So a system optimized for grammatical, coherent output will still admit many failures. This matters even more if reframing is scarce: a filter that rewards only local fluency may preserve mediocre descriptions while missing category shifts.

Now place beside these claims the last two premises: “Compensated chequin intermenstrual trance oxgall” and “Claiborne aphrite micrencephalia apagogical premankind.” They are stipulated true as sentences in the world, but their role is opaque. They are grammatical enough to exist as linguistic objects, yet they do not transparently supply explanatory structure. Their presence demonstrates the previous point by collision: coherence-like form is not enough to guarantee usable scientific content. They also pressure the ontology question. Are these terms names of objects, properties, pathologies, eras, or nothing presently classifiable? If reframing changes what counts as an object or relation, then one possible route to understanding these sentences is not better filtering but a new frame in which they become legible.

So the world implied by the premises is one where progress depends less on polishing sentences and more on changing the map that gives sentences meaning. Search cannot simply shrink; it must sometimes expand to discover a frame under which previously unhelpful strings become structured candidates. Until that reframe occurs, grammaticality and coherence do little, and opaque but true utterances remain as reminders that the scarce act is not saying more, but seeing differently.
